summary: Color is the fastest emotional signal in video. Palette temperature, saturation, and contrast shape how the viewer feels before they process any text or narration.
principles: Every palette has a temperature: warm tones (amber, orange, red) signal comfort, energy, and appetite; cool tones (blue, teal, gray) signal precision, premium, and calm.
Saturation communicates intensity: high saturation = energy, urgency, youth; low saturation = sophistication, heritage, quiet confidence.
Complementary color pairs (opposite on the wheel) create visual tension and attention; analogous palettes (adjacent) create harmony and immersion.
The product color should anchor the palette or intentionally break from it — never drift accidentally.
Color transitions between beats signal emotional shifts: desaturated problem → saturated solution is the most powerful visual before/after.
Background color should support the product, not compete with it. Neutral or tonal backgrounds let the product dominate.
CTA frames should use the highest-contrast color pairing in the palette to maximize visual stop.
apply_when: The brief specifies a mood, tone, or emotional register that color can reinforce.
The brand has a defined color system that must be integrated.
The ad uses a problem-solution arc where visual contrast amplifies the narrative.
avoid_when: The platform or format forces a monochrome or brand-locked template.
Color manipulation would misrepresent the actual product appearance.
The audience includes color-vision-deficient viewers who need luminance contrast rather than hue contrast.
failure_patterns: Rainbow palette with no dominant temperature — the viewer can't settle into a mood and the ad feels chaotic.
Product color lost in the background: the hero product is the same value/saturation as its surroundings, so nothing pops.
Sudden color shift with no narrative reason — jarring the viewer without earning it through the story arc.
Overusing brand blue across every frame: the ad becomes a corporate presentation instead of an emotional experience.

cross_domain_notes.note: Lighting direction and intensity determine which colors read strongest; side lighting warms up, overhead lighting cools down.
Color temperature shifts should track the emotional curve: cool at low intensity, warm at resolution.
Warm saturated visuals pair naturally with warm acoustic textures; cool desaturated visuals pair with clean electronic sound.

execution_techniques: Desaturate everything except the product for instant visual dominance without changing composition.
Shift from cool blues in the problem beat to warm ambers in the solution beat — the viewer feels the shift before they name it.
Use a single accent color from the brand palette on the CTA frame to create visual punctuation.
Match background color value (brightness) to the product so it sits naturally in the scene rather than floating.
